coredata.dc:title: Detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous environment using fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized from endosperm of Borassus flabellifer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: Nagaraj, Murugan
Ramalingam, Srinivasan
Murugan, Chandran
Aldawood, S.
Jin, Jun-O
Choi, Inho
Kim, Myunghee
coredata.dc:description: Natural products derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) catch huge attention owing to their distinctive properties of smaller size, water dispersibility, high photostability, lower cost, tunable emission, biocompatibility, least toxicity, electrical conductivity, optical and catalytic properties, and easy modification. Herein high fluorescent CQDs were prepared using Borassus flabellifer (ice apple) as a carbon source utilizing the simplistic one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared CQDs possessed excellent photoluminescence, high photostability, and stability in an aqueous solution and harbored large of quantum yield and strong stability in high pH conditions with the characteristic strong blue fluorescence emission. With these superior properties, the CQDs have been used as sensing probes for the detection of Fe3+ ions having excellent selectivity and sensitivity with a 2.01 μM limit of detection. The CQDs decorated probe was found effective in detecting Fe3+ ions in the tap and drinking mineral water, suggesting the applicability of the prepared sensor. The developed sensor exhibited advantages, including simple, low-cost, label-free, rapid, and good sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+ ions, with a great application for detection of such ions in real water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: carbon quantum dots
ferric ions (Fe3+)
fluorescent sensor
green synthesis
ice apple
metal contamination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence properties and stability of CQDs
Zeta potential and quantum yield analysis
Selectivity of CQDs with different metal ions
Detection of Fe3+ ions in actual samples

The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Researcher supporting program at King Saud University , Riyadh, for funding this work under the project number ( RSP-2021/328 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis spectrum, inset is the photo image of CQDs solutions (a), FTIR spectrum (b), XRD pattern (c), Raman spectrum (d) of CQDs solution.

The size distribution histogram of CQDs (a), HRTEM micrograph image (b), high-magnification HRTEM image (inset high-resolution HRTEM image of an individual CQD) (c), and schematic diagram of graphitic amorphous CQDs (d).
Full survey XPS spectrum of CQDs (a), high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (b), high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum (c), and high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum (d).

Fluorescence spectra of CQDs solution at different excitation wavelengths (a). Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs at the different excitation wavelengths (b). Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs solution at various storage periods (c). The effect of varying pH on the fluorescence intensity of synthesized CQDs under excitation wavelength at 310 nm (d).
The fluorescence quenching effects of CQDs in the presence of different metal ions (200 μM) (a). A schematic illustration of selective turn-off fluorescence detection of CQDs with Fe3+ ions (b).
Fluorescence spectra of the CQDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ under excitation wavelength at 310 nm (a). The linear calibration plot for different concentrations of Fe3+ (b).
Detection of Fe3+ ions in water by using CQDs as a sensor.

head.ce:title: Detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous environment using fluorescent carbon quantum dots synthesized from endosperm of Borassus flabellifer

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Natural products derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs) catch huge attention owing to their distinctive properties of smaller size, water dispersibility, high photostability, lower cost, tunable emission, biocompatibility, least toxicity, electrical conductivity, optical and catalytic properties, and easy modification. Herein high fluorescent CQDs were prepared using Borassus flabellifer (ice apple) as a carbon source utilizing the simplistic one-step hydrothermal method. The prepared CQDs possessed excellent photoluminescence, high photostability, and stability in an aqueous solution and harbored large of quantum yield and strong stability in high pH conditions with the characteristic strong blue fluorescence emission. With these superior properties, the CQDs have been used as sensing probes for the detection of Fe3+ ions having excellent selectivity and sensitivity with a 2.01 μM limit of detection. The CQDs decorated probe was found effective in detecting Fe3+ ions in the tap and drinking mineral water, suggesting the applicability of the prepared sensor. The developed sensor exhibited advantages, including simple, low-cost, label-free, rapid, and good sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+ ions, with a great application for detection of such ions in real water.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, nanomaterials are gaining high research importance due to their vast application in human life (Manjula et al., 2020; Vijayakumar et al., 2022). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a kind of nanomaterials attaining considerable attention due to their high chemical stability, fluorescent emission properties, water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and stable photoluminescence properties. The easy incorporation of various functional groups on CQDs enables them to attain various physiochemical, functional, and catalytic properties. Different functional groups can be easily used to modify the surface of CQDs, allowing them to have more possibilities for tuning their physicochemical properties to easy functionalization and catalytic properties. These exceptional properties of CQDs provide numerous applications such as cell labeling, drug delivery, detection of transition metal ions, fluorescent inks, photocatalysis, optical imaging, biosensing, and energy conversion/storage applications (Amjadi et al., 2017; Bu et al., 2019). Due to the vast demand for fluorescent probes in biological monitoring, chemical sensing, and other fields, different CQDs synthesis methods such as electrochemical, laser ablation, acid oxidation plasma treatment, thermal oxidation, wet chemical, arc discharge ultrasonic, microwave, and pyrolysis methods have been reported for efficient synthesis (Dong et al., 2012). Nevertheless, these techniques have drawbacks such as multistep fabrication, low yield, complicated setup, time-consuming, high temperature, post-separation process, and high cost (Ma et al., 2019). Recently hydrothermal synthesis is the most commonly used method for CQDs synthesis since it is a simple eco-friendly process, controls the size, purity, low cost, and non-toxic compared to other techniques (Li et al., 2019; Atchudan et al., 2021). Moreover, the green synthesis of CQDs from biological materials is gaining high interest due to the several advantages over synthetic precursors. In general, biomaterials are low-cost and non-toxic renewable raw materials for the synthesis of CQDs (Chahal et al., 2021). Further, using the biomaterials to synthesize CQDs does not need to use toxic solvents and not produce toxic byproducts. Similarly, using natural biomaterials to synthesize CQDs increases the quantum yield, purity of CQDs, and ease of preparation and purification (Chahal et al., 2021).

Currently, raw materials biomass to use as carbon sources to synthesize carbon nanomaterials has received researchers' attention (Shi et al., 2019). Numerous bio-resources such as tomato juice, mango fruit, konjac flour, carrot juice, rice husk, milk, soy milk, apple juice, lemon juice, and prawn shells were used to prepare CQDs (Gao et al., 2016; Gupta et al., 2020; Chahal et al., 2021). However, it is still a challenge to achieve easy preparation (one-step technique) and synthesis of CQDs in plethora from a commercial point of view. Therefore, more facile synthetic methods and green sources to obtain high fluorescent CQDs at the industrial level are preferred. Utilization of biomass from Borassus flabellifer was least explored; thus, the present study used the endosperm of B. flabellifer as a carban source for the synthesis of CQDs, which is not documented yet. Iron is a transition metal and an essential element of life. However, increasing Fe3+ ions in water resources has become a great danger to human health. Several health implications, such as cancer malfunctioning of various organs (heart, pancreas, and liver), have been associated with the increased amount of Fe3+ in the human body (Gao et al., 2015, 2018; Shen et al., 2017). Dissolution of ironstones, industrial wastewater contamination, corrosion of water pipes, and constructional material used in the drinking water resources are significant sources of iron in drinking water. High iron concentration causes turbidity in the water, whereas a low concentration of iron supports the growth of bacteria which clog water supply pipelines consequently make a lousy odor of water. Moreover, a high concentration of iron in the water source is a surrogate for other heavy metals. The abundance of iron in the environment and water resources was much higher than the other heavy metal ions such as lead, mercury, etc. Hence, it is fundamentally significant to monitor Fe3+ in drinking water precisely.

Several methods, traditional and advanced methods like inductive coupled plasma mass spectrometry, electron paramagnetic resonance, atomic absorption spectroscopy, spectrophotometry, electrochemical method, and colorimetric analysis, have been frequently used to detect Fe3+ ions (Andersen, 2005; Matusch et al., 2010; Sussulini et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2017). However, these methods have limitations like the time-consuming, need for costly equipment, laborious experimental conditions, complicated laboratory apparatus, and requirement for skilled technicians (Qi et al., 2019). Thus, there is a need to implement simple, non-expensive, accurate, and fast methods for the detection of iron in the water. With this background, the present study aimed to synthesize CQDs from the endosperm of B. flabellife to analyze Fe3+ ions in tap water and drinking water samples. The current investigation, for the first time demonstrated that the synthesized CQDs from B. flabellife harbor strong fluorescence and remarkable water solubility and, therefore, could be utilized as a promising material for the selective determination of Fe3+ ions.

Aluminium chloride (AlCl3), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), chromium chloride (CrCl3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), cobalt chloride (CoCl2), silver nitrate (AgNO3), copper chloride (CuCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), hydrochloric acid (HCl), iron(II) chloride (FeCl2), iron(III) chloride (FeCl3), lead chloride (PbCl), mercury chloride (HgCl2), nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2), potassium chloride (KCl), and zinc nitrate Zn(NO3)2 were procured from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Distilled water acquired by the Millipore water purification system (Kentwood, MICH, US) was used during the experiments.

A hydrothermal processing method was used to synthesize the CQDs from fruits of B. flabellifer (Li et al., 2019; Atchudan et al., 2021). The fruits of B. flabellifer (ice apple) were obtained from orchards in the Salem district in Tamil Nadu, India. The endosperm (soft jelly in nature) of B. flabellifer fruits were obtained by removing the exocarp, endocarp, mesocarp, and testa of the fruits using a knife. A 10 g of endosperm was smashed to juice in a 100 mL Teflon container, then mixed with 25 mL of double-distilled water, and the Teflon container was kept in a stainless-steel autoclave and closed with a lid. The sealed autoclave was kept into a hot air oven, and heated at 180 °C for 12 h, later allowed to cooled down to room temperature. After the hydrothermal process, the juice of endosperm was turned into a brownish-yellow solution and filtered by using Whatman filter paper (No. 1). The obtained liquid filtrate harboring CQDs was stored at 4 °C for further applications.

The properties of synthesized CQDs were characterized by analyses of their chemical state of elements, elemental composition, physicochemical properties, structure, and surface functionality using various spectral methods; namely, fluorescence spectroscopy (PerkinElmer, Brea, CA, USA), Raman spectroscopy (Horiba Scientific, Rue du Caducee, Montpellier Cedex, France), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Mundelein, IL, USA), Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; JOEL, Tokyo, Japan), X-ray diffraction by using CuKα x-rays (λ = 1.5405 Ao) (XRD; Bruker, Grovewood, Malvern, UK), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Llanthony, Gloucester, UK), and zeta potential analysis (Grovewood, Malvern, UK).

The level of quantum yield in the synthesized CQDs was calculated by an established procedure of the quinine sulfate solution method (Li et al., 2019; Atchudan et al., 2021). Quantum yield (0.54) of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 was severed as a standard. The quantum yield of the CQDs solution was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm, and the yield was calculated using the following formula,
(1)
φ F
=
(
A R
/
A S
)
(
I S
/
I R
)
(
η S
/ η R )
2
φ R
where φ F, A, η, and I were fluorescence quantum yield, optical density, solvent refraction index, and integrated emission area, respectively. The subscript letters ‘S’ and ‘R’ refer to sample and standard reference.

The synthesized CQDs were examined for their selectivity and interferences in different metal ions presence, namely, K+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Na+, Pb2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, and Al3+ at 200 μM concentration. About 2 mL of aqueous CQDs solution (10 μg/L) was mixed with 2 mL of different metal ion salt solutions and incubated at ambient temperature for 1 min. After incubation, the fluorescence intensity was observed at 310 nm (Liu et al., 2017). The high quenching fluorescence metal ions were further determined at lower concentration levels.

The reliability of synthesized CQDs in the practical application was investigated in the drinking water and tap water. The collected tap water (pH – 7.5 and TDS – 235 ppm) and drinking water (pH – 7.2 and TDS – 105 ppm) were filtrated with 0.22 μm (pore size) Whatman filter paper, later centrifuged for 5 min at 10,000 rpm to eliminate residues. The selected water samples were spiked with various known concentrations (10 μM, 20 μM, and 30 μM) of Fe3+ ions. The spiked water samples (1 mL) were mixed with 1 mL of aqueous CQDs solution and incubated for 1 min. After incubation, the fluorescence intensity was read at 310 nm using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Liu et al., 2017).

Fig. 1 a shows UV–visible spectra of the CQDs synthesized from the ice apple. A strong absorption peak was observed at 230 nm in the synthesized CQDs, corresponding to π-π* transition in the aromatic –CC bond. At 306 nm, a weak absorption peak was observed, corresponding to the CC bond. The two peaks are in good agreement for the formation of CQDs. FTIR spectroscopy was used to analyze synthesized CQDs' chemical composition and functional groups information, as shown in Fig. 1b. The major peaks at 3449 cm−1, 2894 cm−1, 1648 cm−1, 1329 cm−1, and 692 cm−1 observed in the FTIR spectrum. The broad-band centered at 3100-3500 cm−1 can be attributed to the vibration of O–H stretching. The main peaks were observed at 1648 cm−1 and 1329 cm−1 for CO stretching vibration and C–O band of –COOH group and 2894 cm−1 for stretching vibrations of C–H group, respectively (Ramanan et al., 2016). In the spectrum pattern, no other secondary peaks were found. The spectrum strongly demonstrated the formation of CQDs from ice apples.

As shown in Fig. 1c, the XRD technique characterized the synthesized CQDs to determine the phase purity and crystal structure. The XRD pattern with two prominent peaks was observed at two theta values of 23.0 and 43.7, respectively, which correspond to the characteristics of the graphitic structure of CQDs. The broad and robust diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.0 was indexed to graphite's (002) reflection plane, representing a d-spacing distance of 0.32 nm. Another peak at 2θ = 43.7 corresponds to C (100) graphitic carbon. All the peaks were consistent with a previous report (Zhao et al., 2019). The XRD peaks of the synthesized CQDs showed broad peaks due to the small-sized CQDs. Fig. 1d shows Raman spectra of the synthesized CQDs. One strong peak observed at 1580 cm−1 was consigned to the graphitic structure with structural defects of sp3 hybridized carbons and referred to as the G band. Another peak at 1330 cm−1 is assigned to the defects and disordered graphite structures designated to the D band sp2 hybridization carbon (Shi et al., 2016). Thus, all these peaks confirm the formation of CQDs from the ice apple.

The microstructure, size and morphology of the produced CQDs were determined by HRTEM (Fig. 2 ). The results of HRTEM images revealed the uniform distribution and spherical shape of the synthesized CQDs, which are amorphous (Lu et al., 2018) (Fig. 2b and c). The size distribution of the CQDs ranges from 2 nm to 7 nm, having a maximum population of 3 nm (Fig. 2a). The inset of Fig. 2c exhibits a crystalline/graphitize morphology with the lattice spacing distance of about 0.21 nm near the graphite (100) plane, which corresponds to the latticed d-spacing of the (100) crystal plane of graphite. The findings of HRTEM were highly correlated with the results of XRD and Raman spectra.

As shown in Fig. 3 , the synthesized CQDs were characterized with XPS for the study of surface functional groups, chemical composition, and elemental states. The CQDs XPS full survey spectrum displayed two distinctive peaks at 533.6 eV, and 286.6 eV ascribed to O1s and C1s elements, respectively (Fig. 3a). The high-resolution C1s spectrum was successfully resolved into 3 individual component peaks centered at 284.5 eV, 285.4 eV, and 286.4eV, (Fig. 3b), which are related to sp2-bonded carbons belonging to hydroxyl (C–OH) and carboxyl groups (COOH), epoxide groups (C–O–C), and carbonyl groups (CO), respectively (Li et al., 2020). Fig. 3c showed that the deconvolution of O1s is resolved into three peaks at 532.2 eV, 533.0 eV, and 533.8 eV, conforming to the C–O, C–O–C, and CO functional groups on the surface of the CQDs, respectively (Atchudan et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 3d, the deconvolution of N1s signal observed at 400.1 eV corresponding to the C–N represents low content carbon bonded nitrogen on the CQDs surface. These results suggest that the synthesized CQDs are rich in carbon, and their functional groups contain oxygen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence properties and stability of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CQDs were further studied for fluorescence properties (Fig. 4 a). The fluorescence spectra were recorded with the augmentation of 10 nm from 300 nm to 390 nm excitation wavelength. Emission (fluorescence) intensity of CQDs initially increased and then decreased. As shown in Fig. 4a the maximum intensity of fluorescence emission was detected at the excitation of 310 nm. The wavelength of excitation has a significant influence on the intensity and emission of the fluorescence of CQDs (Fig. 4b). The emission wavelength shifted to long-range from 395 nm to 486 nm, as an increase in excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 390 nm. This behavior was attributed to the different surface defect states, functional groups, particle sizes, and or various surface energy traps on the surface of CQDs (Xu et al., 2017). The synthesized CQDs did not show any aggregation up to 5 months of the tested period, and this might be due to the electrostatic repulsions of CQDs (Atchudan et al., 2020). Moreover, no significant changes were found in the fluorescence intensity of the CQDs up to 5 months storage period (Fig. 4c). However, during the storage time, a slight fluctuation was found in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs, which agrees with Atchudan et al. (2020) results. The present results suggest that the synthesized CQDs harbor almost constant fluorescence for a minimum of 5 months. Furthermore, the fluorescent properties of CQDs were studied at different pH (3, 5, 7, 9, and 11) (Fig. 4d). Synthesized CQDs are stable in the wide pH ranges from 3 to 11. The maximum fluorescence intensity was found at pH 7, and the same pH was used to detect Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential and quantum yield analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CQDs were further investigated by zeta potential analysis. The CQDs showed a high negative zeta potential value of −38 ± 3 mV. This is because of the negative charge moieties over the CQDs surface. The fluorescence quantum yield is estimated at 19.4%. The quantum yield obtained by this method is higher than earlier reported CQDs in comparison to the other plant biomasses, for example, apple juice (4.2%), winter melon (7.5%), soymilk (2.6%), banana juice (8.9%), pomegranate (7.6%), sugarcane juice (5.6%), cucumber juice (3.2%), carrot juice (5.1%), papaya juice (7%), tomato juice (13.9%), and potato (6.1%). Compared with other natural materials, the quantum yield in the present work was significantly higher than those of the earlier reports (Sharma et al., 2017; Chahal et al., 2021; Meng et al., 2019). In the current report, ice apple-derived CQDs obtained a 19.4% quantum yield, a good figure for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CQDs with different metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of synthesized CQDs was examined using the fluorescence quenching effect of different metal ions, such as K+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Na+, Co2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, and Al3+ metal ion. The intensity of fluorescence was measured after incubation for 1 min, where F0 represents the blank fluorescence intensity, and after adding each metal ion, fluorescence intensity is represented by F (Fig. 5 a). It is shown that Fe3+ had a noticeable fluorescent quenching effect, while remaining metal ions had slight or no interference with fluorescence. These results revealed that the produced CQDs were a highly specific sensitivity towards the Fe3+ ions and can be used as fluorescence sensor probes for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions.

On the surface of the synthesized CQDs, many functional groups like carbonyl groups (CO, C–H), epoxide groups (C–O–C), hydroxyl (C–OH), and carboxyl groups (COOH) are present. The Fe3+ ions only displayed the fluorescence-quenching effect among various metal ions tested. The fluorescence quenching ability of the Fe3+ ions on the surface of the CQDs maybe occur in two ways 1) by energy transfer to the d-orbital of the metal atom from the surface group or 2) by Fe3+ ions chelation on the surface of CQDs. Moreover, the formation of the metal hydroxide complex or other complexes results in the aggregation of CQDs during the treatment of Fe3+ ions (Liu et al., 2020; Lv et al., 2020). The functional groups of synthesized CQDs serve as the electron donors and lead the formation of coordinate bonds with Fe3+ ions; ultimately, the Fe3+ chelates its tapered region on the surface of the CQDs Fig. 5b. It was shown that Fe3+ ions had a noticeable fluorescent quenching effect while other ions had slight or no interference with fluorescence.

The synthesized CQDs sensor probe was studied with various strengths of Fe3+ ion (Fig. 6 ). A Fe3+ ions concentration (10 μM–100 μM) dependent decrease was observed in the fluorescence properties of CQDs due to the formation of (metal) hydroxide complex on the surface of CQDs (Fig. 6a), (Van Tam et al., 2014). Fig. 6b shows the calibration plot between the concentration of Fe3+ and fluoresce intensity, which shows a linear relationship with the regression coefficient (R 2) = 0.9960. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated by using the following formula (Atchudan et al., 2020):
(2)
LOD =
3 σ /S
where S is slope of the calibration plot and σ is the standard deviation of the signals (n = 3). The LOD was noted to be 2.10 μM. The LOD of this study sensor was far lower than that of previously reported CQDs-based sensors such as CDs from cranberry beans 9.55 μM (Zulfajri et al., 2019), blueberry 9.97 μM (Aslandaş et al., 2015), graphene 7.22 μM (Ananthanarayanan et al., 2014), ethylene glycol 7.4 μM (Wang et al., 2017), citric acid and 1-aminopropyl-3-methylimidazolium 13.68 μM (Xie et al., 2017), diammonium hydrogen citrate and urea 19 μM (Khan et al., 2017) anhydrous citric acid and urea 28 μM (Li et al., 2018) α-cyclodextrin 6.02 μM (Hamishehkar et al., 2015) acetonitrile and an ionic liquid 5 μM (Niu et al., 2016), and N-CQDs from L-glutamic acid 4.67 μM (Yu et al., 2016). This advocates that the synthesized CQDs sensor probe can be more efficient than the earlier reported works to detect Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ ions in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The developed sensor probe was evaluated with actual water samples like drinking water and tap water, with which the known concentrations of Fe3+ (10 μM, 20 μM, and 30 μM) were spiked. The spiked samples were vortexed and incubated for 1 min with synthesized CQDs at room temperature and measured the fluorescence excitation intensity at 310 nm. The fluorescence properties of CQDs reduced linearly upon the increment of Fe3+ ions concentration in both the tap water and drinking water. In the present study, satisfactory recoveries were found, i.e., 98.0%, 106.4%, and 101.6% for 10 μM, 20 μM, and 30 μM of Fe3+ ions, with relative standard derivation (n = 3) of 2.01%, 4.50%, and 1.95% in tap water and 101%, 99.5%, and 101.0% with relative standard derivation (n = 3) of 1.48%, 2.04%, and 1.83% in drinking water, respectively (Table 1 ). These analytical results suggest that the fluorescent CQDs derived from ice apple has a strong capability for the application in the fluoresce probe of detecting Fe3+ ions in real water sample analysis.

CQDs were succesfully synthesized from the biomass of B. flabellifer endosperm using a single-step hydrothermal process. The synthesized CQDs showed outstanding fluorescence properties with high quantum yield (19.4%) and good stability (zeta potential of −37.3 mV). The CQDs were amorphous in nature, 2–7 nm in size; however, 3 nm CQDs particles were abundant. CQDs exhibited remarkable fluorescence properties with maximum excitation at 310 nm. The synthesized CQDs had superior sensitivity and selectivity toward Fe3+ ions with a LOD of 2.01 μM, and the linear range of detection was 10–100 μM. The prepared CQDs were efficiently detected the Fe3+ ions in both tap water and drinking water with a minimum of 98% recovery. The findings of the present investigation conclude that the synthesized CQDs had strong fluorescence capacity with good stability, quantum yield, and high sensitivity towards the Fe3+ ions; thus, it could act as a promising sensing material for the selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+ ions in the fluorescence modality.

Murugan Nagaraj and Chandran Murugan conceived the idea and conducted the experimental work. Srinivasan Ramalingam and Nagaraj Murugan wrote the initial draft of the manuscript. S. Aldawood, Jun-O Jin, and Inho Choi reviewed the manuscript. Myunghee Kim evaluated the initial draft of the manuscript, administrated and supervised the project, and finally approved the manuscript for submission. All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation (NRF) of Korea grant funded by the Korean Government (Grant No. NRF-2020R1A6A1A03044512). The authors would like to extend their sincere appreciation to the Researcher supporting program at King Saud University, Riyadh, for funding this work under the project number (RSP-2021/328).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Amjadi et al., 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots as the chemiluminescence probe for detection of Cu2+ ions
J. Lumin.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Amjadi, M., Manzoori, J.L., Hallaj, T., Azizi, N., 2017. Sulfur and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots as the chemiluminescence probe for detection of Cu2+ ions. Journal of Luminescence 182, 246-251.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ananthanarayanan et al., 2014
